lines.text: Hi. I'm the assistant you're about to install.
I'm Personal Jarvis — open source, running on your own computer, and I work by voice.
Give me two minutes. I'll show you how to set me up.
Step one — install me.
One command in your terminal. Windows, Mac, or Linux — I run on all of them.
The first time I start, a setup wizard walks you through everything else.
Step two — bring your own key.
I'm not locked to one AI company. Claude, OpenAI, Gemini, OpenRouter — any single key gets me thinking.
lines.tts: I'm not locked to one AI company. Claude, Open A I, Gemini, Open Router — any single key gets me thinking.

lines.text: Paste it into the API-Keys view — and the dot turns green.
lines.tts: Paste it into the A P I keys view — and the dot turns green.

lines.text: Step three — pick my name.
Choose any wake word you like. That's the word I listen for, all day.
Hey Jarvis.
Yes? I'm listening. That's all it takes.
Step four — just talk to me.
What's on my calendar this afternoon?
lines.tts: Hey Jarvis. What's on my calendar this afternoon?

lines.text: Two meetings. Design review at three, and a call at five.
Hands-free, spoken out loud — and I follow your language: English, German, Spanish, mid-conversation.
Step five — let me use the computer.
Open the browser and check my pull requests.
lines.tts: Hey Jarvis. Open the browser and check my pull requests.

lines.text: I move the cursor, click, type, and read the screen — while you watch every step.
Step six — hand me the big jobs.
Research the EV market and build me a report.
lines.tts: Hey Jarvis. Research the E V market and build me a report.

lines.text: I spawn a background agent for it — and a critic reviews the work before you ever see it.
The finished report lands in your Outputs folder.
That's the whole setup.
I'm free, I'm open source, and everything stays on your machine.
The link's in the description. Say the word.
